title: Add coupon discount support to charge endpoint
summary: This PR introduces coupon discount support but has a critical correctness bug (discount_amount always equals 0), a PCI data leak via console.log of raw card data, silent error swallowing, and FixedCouponStrategy can produce negative charge amounts. Additionally, the code departs from several codebase conventions: the project uses a pino logger with PII redaction throughout, camelCase for all JSON response fields, const/let (not var), and strict equality. The new couponCode field bypasses schema validation and the exported function is named doStuff. There are no tests for any of the new logic.

findings.title: console.log dumps raw card data — PCI violation
findings.message: Line 16 serialises the entire request body — including card.number and card.cvc — to stdout via console.log. The codebase already has a pino logger (src/logger.ts) whose redact config explicitly masks card.number and card.cvc with '[REDACTED]'. This console.log bypasses that protection entirely and will leak PANs and CVCs to wherever stdout is collected (CloudWatch, Datadog, Splunk, etc.). Remove the log entirely; if debugging is needed, use `logger.debug` which respects the redact config.

findings.title: discount_amount is always 0
findings.message: Line 23 sets `payload.amount = finalAmount`, so by line 35 `payload.amount - finalAmount` is always 0 and the response field is useless. Save the original amount before applying the discount: `const originalAmount = payload.amount;` and compute `originalAmount - finalAmount` for the response. The transaction object stored in the DB already reflects the discounted amount, so no DB change is needed — only the response calculation is wrong.

findings.title: Silent catch swallows all coupon errors
findings.message: The inner try/catch around doStuff silently ignores any exception with a bare `// TODO`. If doStuff throws for any reason (including future bugs), the code continues with `finalAmount` still equal to `payload.amount`, applies no discount, and returns no indication to the caller. An unknown coupon code is treated identically to no coupon at all. Either remove the try/catch (let errors propagate to the outer handler), or throw a proper validation AppError for unrecognised codes so the client gets a 400 rather than a silent no-op.

findings.title: FixedCouponStrategy can produce a negative charge amount
findings.message: FixedCouponStrategy.apply returns `amount - this.amt` with no floor at 0. If a customer with a $3 order applies FIVEOFF (500 cents), the result is -200. This propagates to PaymentsService.charge where assertPositiveAmount throws, producing an unhandled 500 instead of a user-friendly validation error. Fix: `return Math.max(0, amount - this.amt)` and add a validation check in the controller (or doStuff) that rejects the request with a 400 if the discount would exceed the order total.

findings.title: couponCode read from raw body before schema validation and echoed in response
findings.message: couponCode is extracted directly from req.body before chargeSchema.parse runs, so it is never validated or sanitised. The raw value is then echoed back in the response as `coupon_code`. Add couponCode as an optional string field to chargeSchema (e.g. `couponCode: z.string().max(32).optional()`) so it is validated and stripped of unexpected characters before use, and so the API contract is explicit.

findings.title: console.log instead of project pino logger
findings.message: The project uses `logger` from src/logger.ts for all structured logging (see errorHandler.ts, paymentsService.ts). The console.log here bypasses structured logging and sends unformatted output to stdout. Replace with `logger.warn({ couponCode }, 'unknown_coupon_code')` — note this also means a valid-looking but unrecognised coupon silently falls through with no discount; that should likely be a 400 validation error instead.

findings.title: response cast to `any` breaks type safety, and fields use snake_case
findings.message: Casting `transaction` to `any` bypasses TypeScript's type system. Construct a properly-typed response object instead: `const response = { ...transaction, discountAmount: originalAmount - finalAmount, couponCode }`. Additionally, `discount_amount` and `coupon_code` use snake_case while every other field in the Transaction type and the entire API uses camelCase (cardLast4, gatewayReference, customerId, createdAt). Use camelCase for consistency.

findings.title: var used instead of const/let throughout couponDiscount.ts
findings.message: Lines 16 and 58 use `var`, and the rest of the file mixes `var` and `let` with no consistency. The rest of the TypeScript codebase uses only `const` and `let`. Replace all `var` with `const` or `let` as appropriate.

findings.title: Loose equality == used for string comparisons
findings.message: All six coupon code comparisons use `==` instead of `===`. TypeScript and the broader JS community standard is strict equality. While this is harmless for string-to-string comparison, it is inconsistent and a linting violation in most TSLint/ESLint configs. Replace all `==` with `===` and `== null` checks with `=== null`.

findings.title: Poor naming: doStuff, tmp, result2, p, amt obscure intent
findings.message: The exported function `doStuff` is meaningless — rename it `applyCoupon`. Internal variables `tmp`, `result2`, `p`, and `amt` are equally opaque. Use `strategy`, `discountedAmount`, `percentage`, and `discountAmount` respectively. Naming matters doubly here because this is a public export used in the controller.

findings.title: No tests for coupon discount logic
findings.message: The PR's own test plan marks unit tests as 'TODO follow-up'. Given that there is already a confirmed correctness bug (discount_amount always 0) and an edge case (negative amounts), this code should not merge without tests covering at least: percentage discount applied correctly, fixed discount applied correctly, discount does not reduce charge below 0, unknown coupon code returns original amount, no coupon code returns original amount, and discount_amount in the response matches the actual reduction. The existing chargeRoute.test.ts is the natural place to add integration-level coupon tests.
